title: Raga Guru Engine
tagline: A real-time pitch analysis and feedback system for Indian classical music practice.

levels.label: Junior
levels.intro: Audio pipeline: pitch detection, raga grammar, and why consumer speech APIs won't work.
levels.theory: Building a raga practice tool requires understanding why speech-to-text APIs and general music recognition services are the wrong tool. (1) Pitch detection for classical music requires sub-semitone resolution — ragas use microtonal intervals (shruti) that differ by 25-50 cents. A standard 12-tone chromatic pitch detector cannot resolve these. YIN algorithm or CREPE (neural pitch estimation) at a minimum 16kHz sample rate are the starting points. (2) Raga grammar is rule-based — each raga has an aroha (ascending scale), avaroha (descending scale), vadi (dominant note), samvadi (second dominant), and characteristic phrases (pakad). Feedback must be grounded in these rules, not generic 'you played the right note'. (3) Latency constraint — practice feedback must arrive within 200ms of a note being played to be useful. Cloud round-trips for audio processing violate this for most connection types. Pitch detection must run on-device. (4) Silence and noise handling — a student pauses, breathes, adjusts their instrument. The pipeline must distinguish intentional silence from noise and not generate feedback during non-performance segments.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Junior theory on pitch detection precision — specifically the microtonal interval requirement and why standard chromatic detectors fail.

levels.label: Senior
levels.intro: Feedback quality: phrase-level analysis, timing, and adaptive difficulty.
levels.theory: Note-level pitch feedback ('you played Sa correctly') is necessary but insufficient for raga learning. Senior-level improvements: (1) Phrase-level analysis — a raga is defined as much by its characteristic phrases (pakad) as by individual notes. The feedback system must detect whether the student is attempting a known pakad, and evaluate the phrase as a unit, not note-by-note. (2) Timing and rhythm — even if every individual pitch is correct, a phrase played at uneven intervals or with wrong meend (glide between notes) is not a correct rendition. Duration analysis of each note and transition must be part of evaluation. (3) Adaptive difficulty — a beginner needs feedback on whether they hit the right note at all. An advanced student needs feedback on the shape of the glide, the sustain, the embellishments. The feedback model must adapt to the student's demonstrated level. (4) Confidence calibration — the system should not give high-confidence 'correct/incorrect' feedback when the audio quality is poor or the note is ambiguous. 'I'm not sure — try again more clearly' is better feedback than a confident wrong evaluation.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Senior theory on phrase-level analysis — specifically how pakad evaluation differs from note-by-note evaluation.

levels.label: Architect
levels.intro: Data and model: training data licensing, cultural sensitivity in automated feedback, model drift.
levels.theory: Automated feedback on Indian classical music carries unique architectural and cultural constraints. (1) Training data licensing — recordings of classical ragas are under copyright, often held by artists or their estates. Training a pitch recognition or phrase evaluation model on unlicensed recordings creates substantial IP exposure. Public domain recordings (pre-1928 in some jurisdictions) and explicitly licensed datasets are required. (2) Cultural sensitivity — Indian classical music traditions (Hindustani vs Carnatic, different gharanas) have genuinely different interpretations of the same raga. A feedback system trained on one tradition's practice may generate incorrect feedback for another tradition's valid interpretation. The system must either scope its domain explicitly or handle tradition-specific rules. (3) Model drift — student performance data accumulated over time is gold for improving the model. But it's also biometric audio data requiring explicit consent, secure storage, and a clear data deletion mechanism. (4) Expert calibration — no automated system should claim to replace a teacher. The feedback must be positioned as a practice aid, with explicit acknowledgment that automated evaluation has known limitations for advanced and edge-case practice.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Architect theory on training data licensing — specifically why using recordings without explicit IP clearance creates exposure.

levels.label: Executive
levels.intro: Market and ethics: preservation vs commercialisation, accessibility, pricing models.
levels.theory: A raga practice tool sits at the intersection of cultural preservation, education technology, and commercial product. (1) Cultural preservation vs commercialisation — Indian classical music is an oral tradition transmitted through the guru-shishya relationship. Building a product that positions itself as a substitute for this tradition risks cultural appropriation and devaluing the transmission relationship. Positioning the product as a supplement that enables more between-lesson practice is ethically defensible; positioning it as a replacement is not. (2) Accessibility — classical music practice tools have historically been inaccessible to students outside major cities or without resources to pay for teachers. An affordable digital supplement addresses a genuine access gap, but pricing must reflect the economic realities of the student population, not just Western tech pricing models. (3) Teacher relationships — the most successful EdTech in this space works with teachers, not around them. A product that teachers recommend is a product that persists; a product that teachers feel threatened by faces structural resistance. (4) Competitive moat — the core IP is the raga grammar database and evaluation model, which requires significant expert input to build accurately. This is a genuine competitive barrier that becomes a moat only if it's built with musician collaboration, not derived from it.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Executive theory on positioning — the distinction between supplement and substitute is the central ethical tension.

tasks.scenario: You're building a raga pitch detector. You test it on a student playing Raga Bhairav. The detector uses a standard 12-tone chromatic pitch library (A4=440Hz, equal temperament). It incorrectly labels Komal Re as out-of-tune rather than a correct microtonal interval. What's the root cause?
options: The student is genuinely playing out of tune in this passage — 12-tone equal temperament is the universal pitch reference that any well-trained ear, human or automated, should be measured against
Equal temperament divides the octave into 12 equal semitones. Indian classical music uses shruti intervals that don't align to it — Komal Re in Bhairav sits where equal temperament reads it as flat
The pitch detection algorithm itself is the problem here — switching to a more accurate algorithm like YIN in place of whatever is currently running would resolve this specific misclassification

The audio is being sampled at too low a rate to capture this level of pitch detail — increasing the sample rate to 44.1kHz would give the detector enough resolution to recognise the microtonal interval correctly
hints: Think about what equal temperament is and what it assumes. Re-read the Junior theory on pitch detection precision and microtonal intervals.

Equal temperament makes all semitones the same size for keyboard compatibility. Indian classical music doesn't do this — it preserves historical just intonation intervals that predate equal temperament standardisation.
tasks.why: Equal temperament is a compromise tuning system designed to make all keys equally usable on fixed-pitch instruments. Indian classical music preserves just intonation intervals where the exact frequency ratios between notes are not equal-tempered. A pitch detector calibrated to equal temperament will systematically misidentify correctly played shruti as out-of-tune. The detector needs to use raga-specific frequency references, not equal temperament.

tasks.scenario: This pitch detection function runs on the server with a 400ms round-trip. Students say feedback comes too late to be useful. Restructure it to run on-device using PyAudio and the YIN algorithm.

checks.label: Uses a local audio processing library
Processes audio locally without a network call
Returns a pitch or frequency value

tasks.why: Feedback latency for a music practice tool must be under 200ms to be actionable — a student can't connect feedback to the note they just played if it arrives half a second later. All pitch detection must run on-device. PyAudio or sounddevice for microphone capture, combined with aubio's yin implementation or librosa's piptrack, can detect pitch with <50ms latency on typical consumer hardware.

tasks.scenario: Put the steps of a raga note evaluation pipeline in the correct order.
items: Apply raga-specific frequency reference table to classify the detected pitch as a valid raga note, out-of-tune, or foreign to the raga
Capture audio frame from microphone at 16kHz+ sample rate
Detect silence/noise: if energy below threshold, skip this frame
Generate feedback based on classification and position in melodic phrase
Run YIN or equivalent pitch detection algorithm on the audio frame
tasks.why: Capture first. Before any expensive processing, check if the frame has enough energy to be intentional performance — processing silence wastes cycles and generates spurious feedback. Pitch detection is next, then classification against the raga's specific note set, then feedback generation which requires knowing both the note and its phrase context.

tasks.scenario: Write the raga grammar definition for Raga Bhupali in a format your feedback engine can use. Include: aroha (ascending scale), avaroha (descending scale), the vadi and samvadi (dominant notes), and one characteristic phrase (pakad).
tasks.placeholder:
Raga Bhupali:
Aroha: Sa Re Ga Pa Dha Sa'...

checks.label: Includes the ascending scale (aroha)
Includes the descending scale (avaroha)
Names the vadi or samvadi
Includes a characteristic phrase or pakad

tasks.why: Raga Bhupali (also called Bhupali or Bhoop) is a pentatonic raga using Sa Re Ga Pa Dha. Aroha: Sa Re Ga Pa Dha Sa'. Avaroha: Sa' Dha Pa Ga Re Sa. Vadi: Ga. Samvadi: Dha. A characteristic pakad: Ga Re Sa, Sa Pa Ga. The feedback engine needs all of these to evaluate not just whether individual notes are correct but whether the melodic movement respects the raga's grammar.

tasks.scenario: Your pitch detector correctly identifies every individual note a student plays. But their Raga Yaman still sounds wrong to any trained listener. The pakad 'Ni Re Ga' is being played as three separate notes with equal duration and no connecting glide. What's missing from your feedback?
options: Nothing is missing here — if every individual note in the phrase is being played and detected at the correct pitch, then by definition the raga is being performed correctly regardless of how it sounds
Phrase-level evaluation is missing: in Yaman, 'Ni Re Ga' requires meend through Re. Note-level accuracy alone can't evaluate transition shape or whether the phrase moves as a connected unit
The student needs to increase their playing tempo through this phrase — at a slower tempo, the kind of glide and ornamentation that defines this phrase isn't actually required to sound correct
Your feedback model simply needs more training examples of correctly performed Yaman recordings — with enough additional data, it will eventually learn to recognise this specific phrase as correct
hints: Think about what defines a raga phrase vs a sequence of correct individual notes. Re-read the Senior theory on phrase-level analysis.

Meend is the defining ornament of many raga phrases — a smooth glide between notes that can cover a major third or more. A system that only checks whether individual notes land at the right pitch cannot detect whether meend is present, correct in shape, or absent.
tasks.why: Note-level pitch accuracy is necessary but not sufficient. Indian classical music phrases are defined by how notes connect, not just what notes appear. Meend (glide/portamento), gamak (oscillation), andolan (slow oscillation on certain notes), and minda (half-glide) are all phrase-level features invisible to a note-level detector. The feedback system must analyse the pitch contour between notes, not just at the note peak.

tasks.scenario: This feedback function gives the same feedback at all skill levels. Modify it to adapt based on the student's demonstrated accuracy over their last 20 attempts.

checks.label: Uses historical performance to adapt feedback
Applies different feedback based on a skill threshold

tasks.why: Adaptive feedback is a core EdTech principle. A beginner who got the right note needs 'Yes' — more information is overwhelming. An advanced student who got the right note but played it too short or without the expected andolan needs specific ornament feedback. Track accuracy rate, note-finding speed, and ornament success over recent attempts. Gate different feedback depth on demonstrated competency thresholds.

tasks.scenario: Your system is not confident about a pitch detection result — the student is playing near the boundary between two microtones, and the confidence score is 0.51. What should the feedback say?
options: Commit to whichever classification has the higher probability and present it as fact to the student — even at 51% confidence, that's still the model's best available guess and should be communicated as such
Display no feedback at all in this situation — when the system itself is this uncertain about a note, saying nothing avoids the risk of confusing the student with an unreliable judgement
Surface the uncertainty: 'I'm not sure about that note — try playing it more clearly or hold it a little longer' — this is better than confident wrong feedback or no feedback
Mark the attempt as incorrect by default whenever confidence falls below a clear threshold — treating uncertain cases conservatively as errors is the safer default for a learning tool to take
hints: Think about what a student can do with 'I'm not sure' vs 'Wrong' or silence. Re-read the Senior theory on confidence calibration.
A student can respond to 'try again more clearly' with a productive action. 'Wrong' when the system is 51% confident leads to a student practising a correction that may not be needed.
tasks.why: Confidence-aware feedback is more useful than confident wrong feedback. When a pitch sits near a classification boundary, the honest feedback is 'unclear — try again.' This lets the student adjust and provides a productive path forward. A 51% confident 'wrong' label causes the student to practise a correction that's only needed 49% of the time. Suppress or qualify low-confidence feedback with a specific actionable suggestion.

tasks.scenario: Write the meend (glide) evaluation criteria for your feedback engine. Describe what makes a meend correct in Raga Bhupali, how you'd detect it in pitch contour data, and what feedback you'd give for a meend that's too abrupt vs too slow.
tasks.placeholder: A correct meend in Bhupali is a smooth glide from...

checks.label: Describes the expected pitch contour shape
States how to detect the meend in pitch data
Distinguishes feedback for different meend errors

tasks.why: A meend evaluation requires: (1) detecting a continuous pitch change (not a jump) between two notes; (2) measuring the rate of change (too fast = glitch rather than meend; too slow = each note separately articulated rather than connected); (3) evaluating the landing — meend should arrive cleanly at the target note, not overshoot or undershoot. Different errors require different feedback: abrupt means 'let the notes connect more smoothly'; too slow means 'the glide should be more fluid, less note-by-note'.

tasks.scenario: You've built a phrase evaluation model trained on 500 hours of Hindustani classical recordings. A Carnatic music student starts using your app and gets consistently poor scores even when their teacher says their practice is excellent. What's the architectural failure?
options: The student simply needs more practice — their scores reflect genuine performance gaps that will close naturally over time as they continue using the app regardless of which tradition the model was trained on

The model was trained on Hindustani tradition. Carnatic music uses different raga grammar, different ornament names and styles, and different melodic conventions for the same named ragas. The model is measuring the student against the wrong tradition's rules

The pitch detection itself is wrong for this student — Carnatic music is performed using a different base reference frequency than Hindustani music, and recalibrating the tonic would resolve the scoring issue

The student's own teacher is simply incorrect in their assessment — a properly trained automated model is generally a more objective and consistent judge of raga performance than an individual human teacher's opinion
hints: Think about what 'raga grammar' means when the same raga name exists in two different traditions with different rules. Re-read the Architect theory on cultural sensitivity.

Raga Bhairavi in Hindustani tradition and Bhairavi in Carnatic tradition share a name but differ in characteristic phrases, ornamentation style, and sometimes note usage. A model trained on one tradition will systematically misgrade valid performances from the other.
tasks.why: Hindustani and Carnatic classical music are distinct traditions with shared roots but different grammars, ornaments, and even different note systems (sargam vs solfège equivalents differ in terminology and sometimes tuning). A model trained exclusively on one tradition cannot evaluate the other. The system must either: (1) scope its domain explicitly and refuse to evaluate the other tradition; or (2) maintain separate grammar databases and evaluation models per tradition, with the student selecting their tradition on onboarding.

tasks.scenario: Your app accumulates student practice recordings to improve the evaluation model. This function saves recordings without consent or deletion mechanism. Fix it to request consent and implement a deletion path.

checks.label: Checks or records consent before saving audio
Implements or references a deletion mechanism

tasks.why: Audio recordings of practice sessions are personal biometric data. Under GDPR, processing biometric data requires explicit consent (Article 9) — not just acceptance of terms of service, but specific consent for this specific processing purpose. The system must also implement the right to erasure (Article 17): a mechanism for users to delete their practice recordings and have them purged from any derived training sets. Record the consent event with timestamp and scope.

tasks.scenario: You want to train a phrase evaluation model on recordings of master musicians to give students a reference to be evaluated against. You've downloaded 2,000 hours of recordings from YouTube. What's the IP exposure?
options: There's no exposure here at all — content hosted publicly on YouTube is by definition freely available for any purpose, training a commercial model included, since the platform itself makes it accessible to anyone

The exposure is limited in this case, since the 'fair use' or fair dealing exception specifically covers educational applications, and a learning tool built for music students clearly falls within that exemption
Limited — the 'fair use' exception covers educational use, so training an educational product is exempt

There's no exposure as long as you don't redistribute the original recordings themselves — training a model on copyrighted material and distributing that resulting model afterwards are legally unrelated, separate acts
hints: Think about what 'publicly accessible' means for copyright. Re-read the Architect theory on training data licensing.

Fair use/fair dealing covers many educational uses but has limits — commercial deployment of a product trained on copyrighted material without licence has been the subject of active litigation in the ML space.
tasks.why: Public accessibility and licence are entirely separate questions. Copyright holders retain control over how their recordings are used regardless of where they're hosted. Training a commercial ML model on copyrighted recordings is an active legal dispute area — in jurisdictions where this has been litigated, courts have not universally accepted 'it's just training data' as a defence. The safe path: use explicitly licensed datasets, recordings from deceased artists where copyright has expired in your jurisdiction, or commission licensed reference recordings from living musicians.

tasks.scenario: Write the model scope disclaimer that must appear in your app explaining what the automated evaluation can and cannot do, particularly in relation to replacing a teacher's feedback.
tasks.placeholder: This practice tool uses automated pitch and phrase analysis to help you...

checks.label: States the tool is a supplement, not a replacement for a teacher
States at least one specific limitation of the automated evaluation
References the role of a human teacher

tasks.why: An automated raga evaluation system must be honest about its scope: it evaluates pitch accuracy and, at best, phrase contour and ornament presence. It cannot evaluate: interpretation, emotional expression (bhava), improvisational choices (manodharma), or the subtle stylistic markers of a gharana. These require a human teacher. The disclaimer must be specific about what the tool does evaluate, not just a generic 'AI has limitations' statement.

tasks.scenario: You're pitching your raga practice tool to a group of music teachers at a classical music academy. Three of the five teachers respond negatively, saying 'this will make students lazy and stop them from coming to classes.' How should this feedback inform your product strategy?
options: Ignore this feedback entirely — teachers have an obvious financial incentive to resist any technology that could reduce demand for the lessons that currently make up their income
This is a structural market signal — teachers are the primary referral channel. A product they feel threatened by faces persistent distribution resistance and needs to be repositioned around them

Address this concern through better marketing messaging aimed directly at teachers — the product's actual benefits to their students simply need to be communicated more clearly and persuasively than they currently are

Find a different target market altogether and go directly to students, bypassing music teachers as a channel completely, since their resistance suggests they aren't the right audience to build trust through
hints: Think about who recommends practice tools to students and what their incentives are. Re-read the Executive theory on teacher relationships.
Teachers are the most trusted recommendation source for music students. A product they actively discourage will face constant headwind regardless of its technical quality.
tasks.why: Teachers are not just stakeholders — in classical music education they are the primary distribution channel for any practice tool. 'Go direct to students' only works if students have discovery and evaluation mechanisms that don't involve their teachers. In classical music, they typically don't. Co-design with teachers (advisory boards, feature input, integration with lesson planning) converts potential opponents into advocates and produces a product that addresses the actual learning gap rather than positioning as a teacher substitute.

tasks.scenario: Write a 2-3 sentence positioning statement for your raga practice tool that: (1) clearly establishes it as a supplement to teacher-led learning, (2) addresses the access gap it solves, and (3) does not overstate the AI's capabilities.
tasks.placeholder: Raga Guru is a practice tool for students of Indian classical music who want to...

checks.label: Positions as a between-lesson supplement
References the access gap
Does not overstate AI capabilities

tasks.why: A positioning statement that says 'replaces your teacher' or 'learn raga without a guru' will be factually wrong, culturally offensive to the tradition, and commercially self-defeating. The honest and defensible position: this tool helps students practise effectively between lessons, get immediate pitch feedback they'd otherwise only get at their next class, and build the muscle memory that makes lesson time more productive — especially for students who can't afford frequent lessons or who live far from qualified teachers.

tasks.scenario: Your raga tool has 10,000 active users. You've accumulated 50TB of practice recordings (with consent) that would be invaluable for training better evaluation models. A large music technology company offers to license this dataset for £2M. What's the primary issue with accepting?
options: None — the consent users gave when signing up for the recording feature already covers this use, since they agreed to have their recordings stored and used to improve the product's underlying technology
The consent collected covered improving your own evaluation model — not commercial sale to a third party. Repurposing biometric audio data beyond that scope is a GDPR breach regardless of price

£2M is too low a price for a dataset of this size and specificity — the right response here is to negotiate a substantially higher fee before agreeing to license the recordings to the technology company

Users have no meaningful expectation of privacy over practice recordings in the first place, since the activity being recorded is a musical performance rather than a private conversation or personal moment
hints: Think about what the consent covered vs what the proposed use requires. Re-read the Architect theory on student data and consent scope.
Consent under GDPR must be specific to the purpose. 'We may use your recordings to improve our product' does not cover 'we may sell your recordings to third parties for their own training purposes.'
tasks.why: GDPR requires that personal data is processed only for the purpose for which consent was given. Consent to use recordings to improve your evaluation model does not cover selling those recordings to a third party for a different training purpose. To accept this offer lawfully, you would need to re-consent every user for the specific new purpose (commercial licensing to a named third party), which they can refuse, and which changes the commercial dynamics of the deal significantly.

tasks.scenario: You're deciding on a pricing model for your raga practice tool. Your target users include: music school students in India (median household income ~£2,000/year), diaspora students in the UK (median household income ~£35,000/year), and professional musicians using it for preparation (variable but high income). What's the right pricing architecture?
options: A single global price set in USD is the right approach — it's the simplest model to operate consistently and keeps pricing uniform and predictable across every market the product is sold into

Purchasing power parity (PPP) pricing: adjust prices to local income levels (India ~85-90% discount vs UK), with separate tiers for students/professionals. A tool priced at UK rates is simply inaccessible to India-based students, which undermines the access mission

The product should be free for every user regardless of region, with the business instead monetising through the value of the accumulated practice recordings rather than through direct subscription revenue

A flat premium price aimed only at professional musicians is the right approach — that segment can clearly afford a higher rate, and their revenue alone can be used to subsidise ongoing product development
hints: Think about who your access gap actually needs to serve vs who can afford to pay. Re-read the Executive theory on pricing and accessibility.
A single global USD price is UK-affordable and India-inaccessible. Free with data monetisation means your business model depends on selling student biometric data. Neither option serves the mission.
tasks.why: PPP pricing is the standard approach for EdTech tools with a genuine access mission. Set prices as a percentage of local median income, not as a flat rate. India-based students pay ~10-15% of UK prices; professionals pay a rate reflecting their use case (preparation, not learning). This serves the access mission without making the product economically unviable. Most payment processors support regional pricing; Stripe in particular has explicit PPP pricing support.
